articles.title: Hola Browser for Windows compromised to deliver cryptominer
articles.summary: The Windows version of the Hola Browser has been compromised in a supply chain attack that delivered an undeclared executable identified by researchers as a cryptocurrency miner. [...]

keypoints: Hola Browser Windows version compromised via supply chain attack
Undeclared cryptominer executable delivered to users

analysis.title: Supply Chain Compromise Vector
analysis.desc: Attackers infiltrated Hola Browser distribution pipeline, injecting malicious cryptomining code into legitimate software updates.

analysis.title: Cryptominer Deployment Method
analysis.desc: Hidden executable silently installed alongside browser, consuming system resources for unauthorized cryptocurrency mining operations.

analysis.title: Detection and User Impact
analysis.desc: Researchers identified undeclared binary; affected Windows users running compromised version unknowingly host malware.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Hola Browser Compromised — Cryptominer Deployed Windows Users
ai_summary.bottom_line: If Hola's on your Windows machine, you're mining cryptocurrency for criminals right now.
ai_summary.action_today: Control Panel → Programs → Uninstall a program → Find Hola Browser → Uninstall immediately

Stolen payment data hosted on legitimate payment processor

analysis.title: Attack Vector Exploitation
analysis.desc: Attackers abuse Stripe's API to deploy malicious checkout code and store exfiltrated card data.

analysis.title: Trust Abuse at Scale
analysis.desc: Using legitimate payment infrastructure masks malicious activity, evading detection on compromised merchant websites.

analysis.title: Checkout Page Vulnerability

articles.title: DentaQuest data breach exposed info of 2.6 million accounts

articles.title: Cisco Patches CVE-2026-20230 in Unified CM as Exploit Code Goes Public
articles.summary: Cisco has patched a bug in Unified Communications Manager that lets an unauthenticated attacker on the network write files to the box and, from there, climb to root. It is tracked as CVE-2026-20230, and proof-of-concept exploit code is already public. Cisco's PSIRT says it has not seen the flaw used in attacks yet. The PoC shortens that runway. The flaw is a server-side request forgery.

keypoints: Unauthenticated attacker can write files and escalate to root
Public exploit code available; patch released by Cisco

analysis.title: SSRF vulnerability in Unified CM
analysis.desc: Server-side request forgery flaw enables unauthenticated network access. Attackers exploit this to write files directly to the system.

analysis.title: Privilege escalation to root
analysis.desc: Initial file-write access chains to root-level compromise. Complete system control possible from network-level attack with no credentials required.

analysis.title: Public PoC accelerates attack timeline
analysis.desc: Proof-of-concept code now public despite no observed attacks. Window for patching before widespread exploitation is narrow and closing fast.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Cisco Unified CM Critical SSRF Vulnerability Patched — PoC Public
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your Unified CM systems are targets right now if unpatched. Attackers have working exploit code.
ai_summary.action_today: Cisco Unified CM → Admin Console → System Updates → Install patch CVE-2026-20230 immediately
